# Coverage option MIR cycle error (2026-02-02 16:20Z)

_**User**_

Why do I get this error when I try to run with the --coverage option:

   Compiling mz-orchestrator-tracing v0.0.0 (/Users/michael.abebe/Code/materialize/src/orchestrator-tracing)
error[E0391]: cycle detected when optimizing MIR for `scalar::func::unary::<impl at src/expr/src/scalar/func/macros.rs:148:53: 148:62>::eq`
   --> src/expr/src/scalar/func/macros.rs:148:53

148 |           #[derive(Ord, PartialOrd, Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize, Hash, mz_lowertest::MzReflect)]
    |                                                       ^^^^^^^^^
    |
   ::: src/expr/src/scalar/func/unary.rs:189:1

189 | / derive_unary!(
190 | |     Not,
191 | |     IsNull,
192 | |     IsTrue,
...   |
514 | |     Reverse
515 | | );

note: ...which requires optimizing MIR for `scalar::<impl at src/expr/src/scalar.rs:52:17: 52:26>::eq`...

            {
              "file": "./doc/developer/design/20240522_mir_representation_types.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 1,
                  "content": "# MIR Representation Types"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 8,
                  "content": "MIR is typechecked using user-facing SQL types."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "This design doc proposes _representation types_---a new notion of type for MIR terms."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 15,
                  "content": "It will also simplify and clarify what MIR is doing."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 42,
                  "content": "2. It should be possible for us to elide noop casts (and possibly others!) at the MIR level."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 49,
                  "content": "We will not rethink other components of MIR types, like nullability or unique keys."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 57,
                  "content": "Having MIR be typechecked in terms of representation types demands a few changes, which can be broken into three PRs:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 60,
                  "content": "2. Have MIR work with &#96;ReprScalarType&#96;."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 182,
                  "content": "- (&#96;eq-type&#96;) Change the MIR typechecker to conflate &#96;varchar&#96; and &#96;text&#96; types (and drop calls to &#96;varchar_to_text&#96;)."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./doc/developer/design/20231213_compute_operator_hydration_status_tracking.md",

                {
                  "lineNumber": 75,
                  "content": "LIDs are assigned during MIR-to-LIR lowering upon creation of each LIR node."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./doc/developer/design/20230829_topk_size_hint.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 168,
                  "content": "min/max aggregates in MIR, but will be subsequently be attached only to hierarchical reductions that"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 506,
                  "content": "semantics to specific SQL clauses, which are then attached to particular HIR and MIR operators."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./doc/developer/design/20230802_explain_running_dataflows.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 256,
                  "content": "  However, the &#96;Coordinator&#96; is required for the global &#96;MIR ⇒ MIR&#96; and the &#96;MIR ⇒ LIR&#96; phases."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 260,
                  "content": "  This is especially problematic for the &#96;MIR ⇒ LIR&#96; lowering and the &#96;LIR ⇒ LIR&#96; transformation phases, which happen in &#96;finalize_dataflow&#96; (see the [Future work](#future-work) section)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 286,
                  "content": "Note that &#96;UsedIndexes&#96; is currently computed inside &#96;optimize_dataflow_index_imports&#96;, which is  called towards the end of the “global” MIR optimization phase in &#96;optimize_dataflow&#96;."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 291,
                  "content": "MIR ⇒ LIR lowering happens in the following stack (in reverse order):"
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./doc/developer/design/20230728_left_join_stack_consolidation.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 148,
                  "content": "Overall, I think the above solution strikes a good balance between complexity and performance considerations. An alternative would be adding an MIR transform to insert ArrangeBys that \"blend in\" with existing ArrangeBys, plus adding an LIR transform that sets a flag on these ArrangeBys to make them also consolidate the raw collection. We discuss this alternative approach in the Future work section below, and we argue that for now the performance benefits of this alternative are not worth the added complexity."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 155,
                  "content": "Examine the usages of &#96;l1&#96; in the above plan: The first usage is the problematic one where we want consolidation. Notice that the second usage is almost immediately arranged (going into a Join), which involves a consolidation. We could conceivably make the needed consolidation \"blend in\" with the arrangement creation of the Join in &#96;l2&#96;. In fact, if an MIR transform inserted an ArrangeBy at the top of &#96;l1&#96;, then the next run of &#96;JoinImplementation&#96; would 1) lift away the Filter that is between the Join in &#96;l2&#96; and the new ArrangeBy, and 2) not insert the ArrangeBy that is currently just below the Join."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 157,
                  "content": "The above observation could solve the consolidation problem with 0 added memory usage, but there would be considerable added complexity. We would need an MIR transform that detects Union-Negate chains without consolidation and inserts ArrangeBys at all but the last Union-Negate. This MIR transform would also need to pay attention that in this case the consolidation shouldn't be right at the top of the Union-Negate, but we need to be mindful of the Join that is hiding behind the second usage of &#96;l1&#96; and place the ArrangeBy in a way that that Join can re-use it."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 161,
                  "content": "- We could have a separate LIR transform that detects a similar Union-Negate pattern as the above MIR transform and tweaks relevant ArrangeBys by adding a &#96;consolidate_raw&#96; flag, which would signal to the rendering that the raw collection in the output of the &#96;ArrangeBy&#96; should be made from an arranged form. However, we generally want to avoid non-trivial LIR transforms."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 162,
                  "content": "- We could add a similar &#96;consolidate_raw&#96; flag on the MIR ArrangeBy to avoid adding a non-trivial LIR transform. However, implementation flags on MIR operators tend to have the problem that it's too much work to keep them from falling off during irrelevant MIR transforms. There is another subtlety with the semantics of this new flag on the MIR ArrangeByt: The problem is that an MIR ArrangeBy is not a guarantee that the output is consolidated even when the next operator consumes the arranged form: when the MIR ArrangeBy is signalling the re-use of an external index, then timestamps are teleported to the &#96;as_of&#96; of the dataflow, and thus we can get unconsolidated data across timestamps that are distinct in the index-creation dataflow but are the same in our dataflow."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./doc/developer/design/20230512_mir_cost_model.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 1,
                  "content": "- Feature name: MIR cost model"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 32,
                  "content": "- Our cost model will be code that runs on actual MIR, producing a symbolic expression that represents the cost of the query along various dimensions. A user can use &#96;EXPLAIN WITH(cost)&#96;  to predict costs."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 114,
                  "content": "  + Cardinality analysis can work on MIR or LIR, since cardinality ought to be constant under optimization."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 120,
                  "content": "  + The memory cost model will work on LIR using the \"lower and check cost\" approach when planning joins in MIR."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 164,
                  "content": "Should the cost model work on &#96;MirRelationExpression&#96; or LIR's &#96;Plan&#96;? Join planning happens at the MIR level, and join planning is a natural first client of the cost model. Even so, join planning is late enough that we could have the cost model work on LIR and plan joins by lowering the joined relations to LIR, considering their cost, and then throwing away that lowering. It's more expensive, but working on LIR means the cost model never has to guess how the query will lower."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 177,
                  "content": "1. **Join ordering.** There are some trade offs here between set enumeration and more heuristic approaches---in an ideal world, we'd have an explicit \"optimization budget\" to determine how much of the join ordering space to explore. (Uses memory at joins; cardinality and predicate selectivity across MIR.)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 179,
                  "content": "2. **CTE filter pushdown.** Pushing filters all the way down can be advantageous, depending on selectivity. (Uses cardinality and predicate selectivity across MIR.) [LDBC BI Q16 is an example that would benefit.](https://github.com/MaterializeInc/materialize/pull/20565#discussion_r1291677593)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 181,
                  "content": "3. **Reduction pushdown.** Pushing reductions down is not always advantageous. (Uses cardinality and predicate selectivity across MIR.)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 183,
                  "content": "4. **Late materialization.** Given a join on a number of tables, it may be advantageous to break the join in two: a small join on a projection to set of common keys and a larger join to collect the other fields. Whether or not late materialization is worthwhile depends on which arrangements already exist. (Uses memory and predicate selectivity over MIR (or maybe LIR).)"
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./doc/developer/design/20230421_stabilize_monotonic_select.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 40,
                  "content": "The MVP PR MaterializeInc/materialize#18546 exploited the fact that one-shot &#96;SELECT&#96;s are almost monotonic, i.e., already exhibit logical monotonicity, to improve their plans. Each &#96;Plan&#96; LIR operator that has a monotonic variant, namely hierarchical reduction and top-k, is given the possibility to convert a non-monotonic operator &#96;Plan&#96; into a monotonic one with forced consolidation, represented by a &#96;must_consolidate&#96; flag. The rendering of these LIR operators then respects the &#96;must_consolidate&#96; flag by either introducing a &#96;consolidate_named&#96; or not on the key-value input obtained after computing group keys and relevant values. Additionally, the MVP PR exposed the separate stages of &#96;finalize_dataflow&#96; through &#96;EXPLAIN OPTIMIZER TRACE&#96;, including MIR-to-LIR lowering and plan refinements for source MFPs as well as for single-time monotonic operator selection."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 42,
                  "content": "## Relationship to Monotonicity Analysis in MIR and &#96;EXPLAIN&#96;"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 46,
                  "content": "Since monotonicity analysis is unchanged in MIR, in the case of one-shot &#96;SELECT&#96;s, lowering to LIR first considers only source monotonicity as derived by &#96;MonotonicFlag&#96; for operator selection. Importantly, during lowering, it is thus safe to set the &#96;must_consolidate&#96; flag of monotonic LIR &#96;Plan&#96; operators to &#96;false&#96;, since the &#96;MonotonicFlag&#96; analysis asserted that the input stream at that point in the plan respects physical monotonicity. Subsequently, a plan refinement pass in LIR is performed where the operator selection is revisited if the dataflow operates on a single time. In this case, only logical monotonicity can be asserted, and thus it is necessary to set &#96;must_consolidate&#96; to &#96;true&#96; whenever an LIR &#96;Plan&#96; operator is converted to its monotonic variant."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 58,
                  "content": "When physical monotonicity originating from monotonic sources is recognized in MIR, the lowering to LIR sets the &#96;must_consolidate&#96; flag of relevant &#96;Plan&#96; nodes to &#96;false&#96;. Since &#96;consolidate_name&#96; operators will reduce the input size by aggregating updates to the same rows, this reduction comes at the cost of potentially significant memory allocation in the form of an arrangement, proportional in size to the number of distinct rows. So recognizing physical monotonicity and skipping consolidation can result in saving potentially significant memory as well as avoiding unnecessary overhead. In single-time dataflows, one example where operator output becomes physically monotonic is for &#96;Plan::Reduce&#96;. Reductions never emit retractions in a single-time context. Thus, if their output is given as input to a monotonic LIR &#96;Plan&#96; operator, then the corresponding &#96;must_consolidate&#96; flag could be set to &#96;false&#96;. The LIR plan refinement step for single-time dataflows needs to be evolved to recor [... omitted end of long line]"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 113,
                  "content": "The logic in the analysis above is similar to what is implemented at MIR level in the &#96;MonotonicFlag&#96; analysis. However, we consider only single-time contexts and a different level of abstraction, since the analysis is performed in LIR."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 123,
                  "content": "Instead of breaking down the single-time LIR-based refinement step into two sub-actions, we could try to achieve both replacement of monotonic plan nodes and correct setting of their &#96;must_consolidate&#96; flags in a single action. This increases the complexity of the code, as analysis of subgraphs needs to be part of applying the action recursively to a given &#96;Plan&#96; node. To avoid the a recursive &#96;Plan&#96; traversal during plan refinement, an alternative would be to integrate the consolidation analysis during MIR-to-LIR lowering itself; however, that would polute the lowering code with considerations only applicable in single-time contexts. In the future, we may be possible to rework the present code by applying the &#96;Interpreter&#96; structure also during lowering."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 151,
                  "content": "We might want to rethink in future work the relationship between monotonicity analysis in MIR and the single-time monotonicity analysis that will facilitate refinement of consolidation in LIR. It feels odd to have two monotonicity analysis passes that achieve slightly different objectives with somewhat similar code, but at different IR levels of abstraction. But we might consider this acceptable as the analyses take place at different levels of abstraction."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./doc/developer/design/20230330_recursion_limit.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 70,
                  "content": "At the AST and HIR levels we can simply add a field to our WMR block representation. However, in MIR we have the problem that if we simply put one limit into each WMR block, then limit settings would not survive &#96;NormalizeLets&#96;. This is because &#96;NormalizeLets&#96; sometimes merges WMR blocks (and moves bindings around in some other ways). For example, if there are two independent WMR clauses (whose results eventually flow together into, e.g., a union or join), then &#96;NormalizeLets&#96; merges these two WMR blocks. In this case, setting just one limit of this merged WMR clause would be wrong."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 72,
                  "content": "To solve the above problem, I propose to have a per-let-binding limit in MIR and LIR (and in rendering), so that different let bindings that came from different WMR clauses with differing limits could retain their differing limits. This needs some improvements in &#96;NormalizeLets&#96;:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 84,
                  "content": "We should make MIR EXPLAIN specially handle the case when all bindings of a &#96;LetRec&#96; have the same limit, and print the limit simply on the &#96;LetRec&#96; in this case, to avoid confusing users in simple cases."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./doc/developer/design/20230223_stabilize_with_mutually_recursive.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 47,
                  "content": "6. MIR generalization (&amp;check; in MaterializeInc/materialize#16561)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 48,
                  "content": "7. MIR optimization corrections (focus of this document)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 54,
                  "content": "1. Complete TODOs that fall under the \"MIR optimization corrections\"."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 257,
                  "content": "1. We have enabled sufficient MIR transformations to not feel horrible about the optimization opportunities that are lost in a WMR context."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./doc/developer/design/20230209_invalid_accumulations.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 77,
                  "content": "Regarding point (1), the most promissing option was to implement the post-processing as a map-project chain with the relevant error processing code encoded as &#96;MirScalarExpr&#96;. This option embraces error handling from invalid accumulations in reductions as a semantic, query-level construct, but it may suffer from lower expression evaluation performance than rendering-level alternatives. Importantly, since MIR transformations eventually reason about error-freeness of expressions, it is necessary to introduce the map-project chain at a higher level of abstraction. It was argued, in particular, that this enriched reduce translation should be introduced at the level of HIR-to-MIR lowering. At this point in the query optimization pipeline, all aggregate functions have already been appropriately detected and collected into &#96;Reduce&#96; nodes. Thus, we do not need to reason about query nesting or how to rewrite multiple interrelated aggregates with different placements in a query (e.g., consider d [... omitted end of long line]"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 81,
                  "content": "In more detail, this approach comprises first redefining the meaning of MIR &#96;Reduce&#96;, denoted &#96;Reduce^{MIR}&#96;, to include an additional aggregate validity column. That is, the output schema of &#96;Reduce^{MIR}&#96; is now defined as:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 84,
                  "content": "Output_Schema(Reduce^{MIR} {key, aggrs, monotonic, expected_group_size} (input^{MIR})) ="
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 90,
                  "content": "Then, we translate HIR &#96;Reduce&#96;, denoted &#96;Reduce^{HIR}&#96;, as a map-project chain applied over &#96;Reduce^{MIR}&#96;, namely:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 93,
                  "content": "[[Reduce^{HIR} {key, aggrs, expected_group_size} (input^{HIR})]]^{MIR} ="
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 94,
                  "content": "    Project^{MIR} {0, ..., k-1, k+l+1, ..., k+2l+1} ("
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 95,
                  "content": "        Map^{MIR} {output_or_error(k, k+l), ..., output_or_error(k+l-1, k+l)} ("
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 96,
                  "content": "            Reduce^{MIR} {[[key]]^{MIR}, [[aggrs]]^{MIR}, false, expected_group_size} ([[input^{HIR}]]^{MIR})"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 101,
                  "content": "Since the translation is performed during HIR-to-MIR lowering, the query optimizer will have the opportunity to apply all relevant MIR transformations to the expression produced. In particular, the map and projection can be hoisted so that the arrangement produced by the reduction can be reused by other operators in the query, e.g., a join. With such an optimization, we would delay producing errors in favor of minimizing memory footprint. Importantly, since this optimization already exists, it would be applied without requiring any specific knowledge that the error expression was introduced for checking invalid accumulations in reductions, fitting naturally into the query optimization pipeline. However, other optimizations might need to recognize this pattern, a risk that was deemed substantial."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 103,
                  "content": "To map out the latter risk, two initiatives were pursued. Firstly, we implemented a fix to the Category 2 panics for &#96;uint2&#96; and &#96;uint4&#96; sum aggregates using this approach, but without adding an additional column out of the reduction operator. In particular, the introduction of a post-processing step after aggregation that performs an appropriate [cast operation](https://github.com/MaterializeInc/materialize/blob/d28272444db09053e89eab1d568ba3a81f3da19a/src/expr/src/scalar/func.rs#L3868-L3870) was pursued. This more specialized version of the HIR-to-MIR translation approach was illustrated in PR [#17709](https://github.com/MaterializeInc/materialize/pull/17709), which got merged, but eventually undone by the reduction pair approach argued for in the main part of this document."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 105,
                  "content": "Secondly, we tried to estimated the impact on optimizer transforms of potentially extending MIR reductions to validating reductions by the translation approach. This impact assessment was documented as PR [#17872](https://github.com/MaterializeInc/materialize/pull/17872), which was not merged. There, we find that several transforms would need to be reworked to account for the extra column produced as part of the proposed translation. While some changes are simple, some others carry more complexity, e.g., in &#96;reduction_pushdown&#96;. Some transformations, such as &#96;reduce_fusion&#96; and &#96;threshold_elision&#96;, might become ineffective with the additions of the map-projection chains to validating reductions and would need to be significantly reworked."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./doc/developer/design/20221204_with_mutually_recursive.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 28,
                  "content": "6. MIR generalization"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 29,
                  "content": "7. MIR optimization corrections"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 155,
                  "content": "6.  MIR generalization"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 157,
                  "content": "    MIR also requires a multi-binding &#96;Let&#96;, which could be prototyped as &#96;LetRec&#96; or &#96;MultiLet&#96;."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 159,
                  "content": "7.  MIR optimization corrections"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 161,
                  "content": "    All MIR optimizations are at risk of being incorrect with recursive &#96;Let&#96; bindings."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./doc/developer/101-query-compilation.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 18,
                  "content": "* [&#96;MIR&#96;](https://github.com/MaterializeInc/materialize/blob/main/src/expr/src/relation/mod.rs) — mid-level intermediate representation."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 39,
                  "content": "* [&#96;HIR ⇒ MIR&#96;](https://github.com/MaterializeInc/materialize/blob/main/src/sql/src/plan/lowering.rs)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 47,
                  "content": "* [&#96;MIR ⇒ MIR&#96;](https://github.com/MaterializeInc/materialize/blob/main/src/transform)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 70,
                  "content": "* [&#96;MIR ⇒ LIR&#96;](https://github.com/MaterializeInc/materialize/blob/main/src/compute-client/src/plan/mod.rs#L882-L897)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 72,
                  "content": "        * All aggregations are created equal in MIR, but from the rendering perspective, [aggregations are evaluated differently according to what data needs to be kept to recalculate the aggregation after receiving a diff](https://github.com/MaterializeInc/materialize/blob/main/src/compute-client/src/plan/reduce.rs). A pictorial version can be found [here](https://github.com/MaterializeInc/materialize/blob/main/doc/developer/arrangements.md)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 78,
                  "content": "For a one-off query, we run all the transformations until the MIR stage. Then we"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 81,
                  "content": "the query on the \"slow path\", then we can skip the &#96;MIR ⇒ LIR&#96; and the &#96;LIR ⇒ TDO&#96; steps."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 86,
                  "content": "Currently, the optimization team is mostly concerned with the &#96;HIR ⇒ MIR&#96; and &#96;MIR ⇒ MIR&#96; stages."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 111,
                  "content": "    * [There is a DSL to specifying arbitrary MIRs.](https://github.com/MaterializeInc/materialize/tree/main/src/expr-test-util)"

| Web search results for query: rust-lang rustc issue E0391 cycle MIR optimization derive |  | Links:<br/>1. [Error codes index](https://dev-doc.rust-lang.org/error_codes/E0391.html)<br/>2. [Error codes index](https://doc.rust-lang.org/error_codes/E0391.html)<br/>3. [Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests...](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/125867)<br/>4. [MIR Optimizations (mir-opt) Working Group](https://rust-lang.github.io/compiler-team/working-groups/mir-opt/)<br/>5. [Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests...](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/119502)<br/><br/># E0391: Type Dependency Cycle in Rust<br/><br/>**Error Definition:** E0391 is a rustc error that occurs when a type dependency cycle is encountered.[1][2] This happens when types, traits, or other declarations have circular dependencies on each other.<br/><br/>**Common Example:** The classic case involves circular trait dependencies where `FirstTrait` depends on `SecondTrait`, which itself depends on `FirstTrait`.[1][2]<br/><br/>**Real-World Case:** E0391 can occur in unexpected situations, such as when using `tokio::fs::File` incorrectly—instead of showing the expected function signature error, the compiler detects a cycle during type resolution that masks the actual diagnostic message.[3]<br/><br/>**Related Issues:** E0391 cycles can also be triggered when checking effective visibility or during MIR (Mid-level Intermediate Representation) optimization phases, where the cycle detection sometimes hides more relevant error messages.[5]<br/><br/>**Learning Resources:** For more information on how cycles are handled in the compiler, see the rustc-dev-guide sections on [queries](https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/query.html).[1][2] |
